chunk_summary: The ten-step WHO care model for SAM management emphasizes inpatient stabilization and outpatient rehabilitation, focusing on preventing early deaths and ensuring catch-up growth through structured care phases.
learning_objectives: Understand the ten-step WHO care model for managing severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Differentiate between inpatient stabilization and outpatient therapeutic feeding for SAM.
Learn the phased approach to SAM care, including early stabilization and catch-up growth.
Recognize the importance of preventing iatrogenic complications in SAM management.
key_facts: Early deaths in SAM are often iatrogenic, caused by fluid and feeding errors.
The ten-step model includes treating hypoglycemia, hypothermia, dehydration, and infections, and correcting electrolyte and micronutrient imbalances.
Feeding begins with F-75 (small, frequent feeds) during stabilization and transitions to F-100 or RUTF for catch-up growth.
Day 0–2 focuses on steps 1–7 (stabilization), day 3–7 transitions to step 8, and week 2 onward emphasizes sustained growth and outpatient follow-up.
Iron supplementation is delayed until the rehabilitation phase to avoid complications.
reference_quote: Early deaths in SAM are often iatrogenic (fluid and feeding errors) and danger signs may be muted. (Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK154454/)
